content.text: Compliance approval gates are structured decision points embedded in the delivery pipeline of an agentic AI system — checkpoints where automated policies, security scans, and human reviewers must each confirm that the system meets regulatory, legal, and organizational requirements before the artifact proceeds to the next stage or enters production. Unlike conventional software gates that focus on correctness and performance, compliance gates specifically address data governance, liability, fairness, and regulatory exposure — areas where a defect can result in regulatory action, not merely a bug report.

attrs.alt: Compliance approval gate pipeline: automated policy check, security scan, legal/human review, runtime monitor, and risk-based gate activation for low, medium, and high risk AI systems

content.text: is a blocking step in a CI/CD or deployment workflow that evaluates an AI artifact — model weights, prompt configuration, inference service, or agent workflow definition — against a defined compliance specification. The gate either passes the artifact (allowing it to proceed) or blocks it (halting the pipeline), optionally with an escalation path for human review.
Gates are composed from three primitives:

— a machine-readable rule set (e.g., OPA Rego, AWS Config rules, a custom Python assertion) that evaluates artifact metadata, environment configuration, or model behavior reports against compliance criteria.

— the structured artifacts produced by a gate evaluation: a SBOM (software bill of materials), a DPIA (data protection impact assessment), model cards, bias evaluation reports, scan results. Evidence is persisted to an audit store.
— an immutable entry in an audit log recording who or what evaluated the gate, the pass/fail outcome, the evidence hash, and a timestamp. Required for regulatory traceability.
Gates operate at two lifecycle points:
(before an artifact reaches a target environment) and
(continuously monitoring a running system for compliance drift, such as PII leaking into logs or model behavior deviating from the approved specification).

Agentic AI systems introduce compliance risks that traditional software does not. An agent can autonomously take actions — sending emails, querying databases, calling external APIs, modifying documents — and its behavior emerges from a combination of model weights, prompts, tool definitions, and runtime context. Regulatory frameworks explicitly recognize this shift.

classifies AI systems into risk tiers (unacceptable, high, limited, minimal). High-risk systems — including those used in hiring, credit scoring, biometric identification, healthcare decisions, and critical infrastructure — must undergo a
before market placement (Article 43) and maintain logs sufficient to enable post-market monitoring (Article 12). Operators of high-risk systems bear affirmative obligations.

automated decision-making that produces legal or similarly significant effects on individuals requires a lawful basis under Article 22. Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) are mandatory before processing that is likely to result in high risk (Article 35). Agentic systems that retrieve, summarize, or act on personal data trigger these requirements.

any AI agent handling protected health information (PHI) must implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards (45 CFR Part 164). Compliance gates must verify that PHI is not retained in model logs, that API calls are covered under a Business Associate Agreement (BAA), and that access controls are documented.

the Trust Services Criteria require evidence of change management controls. A compliance gate that blocks deployments lacking an approved change ticket and produces a signed deployment record contributes directly to the audit evidence set.

model risk management frameworks require validation of model assumptions, performance testing, and independent review before a model is deployed for business decisions. Agentic systems operating in trading, lending, or customer service contexts fall under these rules.
Without gates, compliance evidence is assembled retrospectively — after an audit demand or incident — which is both expensive and unreliable.
defects identified at the gate cost far less to remediate than those discovered during a regulatory audit or post-incident review.
every gate evaluation produces an immutable record. The audit trail can be produced on demand to regulators without manual reconstruction.
demonstrable process — policy written as code, evidence collected automatically — shows due diligence even when a violation occurs.
automated gates filter out obvious violations so human reviewers see only cases that genuinely require judgment.
policy encoded in OPA or a custom check is applied identically to every deployment, eliminating the variance introduced by manual checklists.
when frameworks like the EU AI Act require a technical file (Article 11), gate evidence packages can populate it automatically.
gates externalize compliance requirements as code, giving engineering teams a clear and testable specification rather than ambiguous policy documents.
risk-based gating applies heavier scrutiny to high-risk systems and lighter scrutiny to low-risk ones, avoiding one-size-fits-all overhead.
human review gates introduce waiting time (hours to weeks depending on SLA). This is inherent when meaningful human judgment is required.
automated policy checks can block legitimate deployments on technicalities. Policy maintenance is ongoing work.
each compliance domain (security, privacy, fairness, legal) tends to add gates independently. Without a unified gate orchestrator, the pipeline becomes difficult to reason about.
producing and retaining signed artifacts for every deployment adds storage and key management overhead. For frequent CI builds, evidence should be scoped to promotion events (not every commit).
regulatory requirements evolve. Gate policies must be updated when regulations change, which requires its own change management process.

automated gates can only check what they can measure. Emergent behaviors of LLM-based agents (hallucination, jailbreaking, unexpected tool use) require behavioral testing that is probabilistic, not deterministic.

A compliance gate is triggered by a pipeline event — a code push to a protected branch, a container image promotion to a staging registry, or a scheduled compliance re-evaluation. The gate executor runs a sequence of checks, collects evidence, and records a decision.
Pipeline event triggers the gate (e.g., a merge to
— the exact model version, prompt hash, and container image digest being evaluated.
The executor calls one or more
(OPA, custom scripts) with the artifact metadata as input.
For each check, the executor collects
(scan output, DPIA PDF, model card JSON) and computes a content hash.
All evidence hashes are written to a
(e.g., Rekor, a WORM-compliant S3 bucket) alongside a signed decision record.
If all automated checks pass and no human gate is required (low risk), the artifact is promoted.
If a human gate is required, the executor opens a
(Jira ticket, GitHub PR review, or a dedicated compliance portal) and waits for an approval signal.
Upon receiving the approval signal (webhook, API call, or manual trigger), the gate updates the decision record and releases the promotion lock.
If any check fails or the reviewer rejects, the pipeline is blocked and the decision record captures the rejection reason.
A production-grade compliance gate system has five components:

the CI/CD job (GitHub Actions step, Jenkins stage, Tekton task) that sequences gate checks and enforces the pass/fail decision. It reads gate configuration from a manifest that maps artifact risk level to required gate types.

evaluates artifact metadata against rules. Open Policy Agent (OPA) with Rego policies is the most common choice because policies are declarative, testable, and version-controlled alongside application code.

an append-only storage system for compliance artifacts. For regulated industries, this must be tamper-evident — a WORM (Write Once Read Many) bucket, a transparency log (Sigstore Rekor), or a blockchain-anchored ledger.

a system for routing manual gate decisions to the appropriate reviewer and capturing their decision with provenance (who approved, when, under which authority). Commonly implemented via PagerDuty, Jira Service Management, or a bespoke compliance portal.

a read-side view aggregating gate decisions, pass rates, open human reviews, and time-in-gate metrics. Tools like Grafana, Datadog, or a compliance-specific platform (Vanta, Drata) consume decision records from the evidence store.
any agentic system used in employment, credit, education, law enforcement, or healthcare decisions requires conformity assessment before market placement.
systems handling personal data of EU/UK residents require DPIAs before deployment (GDPR Article 35), making a compliance gate the natural enforcement point.
agentic systems providing investment advice, credit decisions, or fraud detection must satisfy model risk management requirements (SR 11-7 in the US, SS1/23 in the UK).
any system processing PHI or generating clinical recommendations must pass HIPAA safeguard checks and, for SaMD, FDA regulatory requirements.
when customer contracts include compliance commitments (SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA BAA), gates ensure every deployment update preserves those commitments.

runtime compliance gates apply when the system's data processing could drift (e.g., an agent accumulates PII in a vector store that was initially empty, or a model update changes output characteristics).

Lower-risk use cases — an internal search assistant operating on public documentation with no personal data — typically do not require full gate stacks, though automated security scans and basic policy checks remain good practice.
Implementation proceeds in four stages: risk classification, policy authoring, gate integration, and evidence management.

Define a risk register that maps system characteristics to a risk tier (low/medium/high). The classification determines which gates are mandatory. Key attributes: data sensitivity (PII, PHI, financial data), decision impact (informational vs. consequential), user population (internal vs. public vs. vulnerable groups), and regulatory jurisdiction.

Author policies as code in OPA Rego or a Python-based policy framework. Each policy takes artifact metadata (model card JSON, environment config, SBOM) as input and returns a structured decision with a reason.
Wire the policy engine and evidence collection into a CI/CD step. The step fails the build if the gate returns a violation, which blocks promotion to the target environment.

# .github/workflows/compliance-gate.yml
name: Compliance Approval Gate

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      risk_tier:
        description: "Risk tier override (low/medium/high)"
        required: false

jobs:
  compliance-gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      id-token: write    # For OIDC-based evidence signing

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install opa-python-client requests boto3

      - name: Collect artifact metadata
        id: metadata
        run: python scripts/collect_artifact_metadata.py
        # Writes artifact_metadata.json with model card hash,
        # SBOM, DPIA status, environment config, review status.

      - name: Run OPA compliance check
        id: opa_check
        run: |
          python scripts/run_compliance_gate.py             --policy policies/ai_deployment.rego             --input artifact_metadata.json             --output gate_decision.json

      - name: Upload evidence to audit store
        if: always()
        run: |
          python scripts/upload_evidence.py             --decision gate_decision.json             --artifacts artifact_metadata.json             --bucket ${{ vars.COMPLIANCE_EVIDENCE_BUCKET }}             --sign

      - name: Fail on violations
        run: |
          python -c "
          import json, sys
          d = json.load(open('gate_decision.json'))
          if not d['allow']:
              print('COMPLIANCE GATE FAILED:')
              for v in d['violations']:
                  print(' -', v)
              sys.exit(1)
          print('Gate passed.')
          "

# scripts/run_compliance_gate.py
"""
Evaluates a compliance policy against artifact metadata using OPA REST API
and writes a structured gate_decision.json.
"""
import argparse
import json
import subprocess
import hashlib
import datetime
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def compute_sha256(path: str) -> str:
    h = hashlib.sha256()
    h.update(Path(path).read_bytes())
    return h.hexdigest()


def evaluate_with_opa(policy_path: str, input_data: dict) -> dict:
    """
    Runs OPA eval via subprocess (opa binary must be on PATH).
    Returns the policy result document.
    """
    result = subprocess.run(
        [
            "opa", "eval",
            "--data", policy_path,
            "--input", "/dev/stdin",
            "--format", "raw",
            "data.ai.compliance",
        ],
        input=json.dumps(input_data).encode(),
        capture_output=True,
        timeout=30,
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        raise RuntimeError(f"OPA eval failed: {result.stderr.decode()}")
    return json.loads(result.stdout)


def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--policy", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--input", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--output", required=True)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    input_data = json.loads(Path(args.input).read_text())
    policy_result = evaluate_with_opa(args.policy, input_data)

    decision = {
        "evaluated_at": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
        "policy_hash": compute_sha256(args.policy),
        "input_hash": compute_sha256(args.input),
        "allow": policy_result.get("allow", False),
        "violations": policy_result.get("violations", []),
        "risk_tier": input_data.get("system", {}).get("risk_tier", "unknown"),
    }

    Path(args.output).write_text(json.dumps(decision, indent=2))
    print(json.dumps(decision, indent=2))


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Three scenarios illustrating gate behavior at different risk tiers:

An internal knowledge-base assistant that indexes public engineering documentation. Risk tier: low. Required gates: automated policy check (data residency, model card), security scan (SAST, SCA, secrets detection). No human review gate. Cycle time: 8 minutes. The gate confirms the system does not process personal data and the container image has no known CVEs above CVSS 7.0.
Scenario B — Customer-facing agent handling PII

A customer support agent that reads order history and account details. Risk tier: medium. Required gates: automated policy check (confirms DPIA is present and signed, data is processed in approved EU regions, logs are masked at the PII fields), security scan, DPO review gate (SLA: 5 business days). The DPO review gate opens a Jira ticket in the DPO's queue; the pipeline waits for an
Scenario C — High-risk hiring decision system

An agent that screens CVs and ranks candidates for shortlisting. Risk tier: high under EU AI Act Annex III. Required gates: automated policy check (DPIA confirmed, bias evaluation report present and within acceptable thresholds for demographic parity difference), security scan, legal counsel review (mandatory for EU AI Act Article 43 conformity), DPO review, executive sponsor sign-off. Estimated cycle time: 2–4 weeks for initial deployment. The gate produces a full technical file package (Article 11) as evidence.

A European bank deploying an agentic loan assessment system operates the following gate pipeline, driven by regulatory obligation under the EU AI Act (high-risk, Annex III point 5b — creditworthiness assessment) and EBA guidelines on internal governance:

OPA verifies that the model card is present, signed by the model risk team, and includes a fairness evaluation on demographic parity difference across protected attributes (< 0.05 threshold). Also checks that training data lineage is documented and that the model is not marked as experimental.
Gate 2 — Security and supply chain scan:

Trivy scans container images for CVEs; Syft generates an SBOM; Gitleaks checks for secrets in model configs. Any CVSS ≥ 9.0 CVE blocks immediately; CVEs between 7.0 and 8.9 require documented risk acceptance from the CISO.

the bank's Model Validation unit reviews the model card, backtesting results, and stress test outputs. They apply SR 11-7 and local EBA guidelines. SLA: 10 business days. Their sign-off is captured in the internal GRC system and webhook-notified to the pipeline.

the Data Protection Officer confirms the DPIA is complete and the processing can proceed under GDPR Article 6(1)(b) (contractual necessity) with Article 22(2)(a) exception. SLA: 5 business days after model validation completes.

the compliance officer confirms the EU AI Act technical file is complete (system description, data governance documentation, conformity assessment record, post-market monitoring plan). SLA: 3 business days after DPO review.

a sidecar process continuously checks inference logs for PII leakage, monitors output confidence distributions for drift from the approved baseline, and alerts if demographic disparity in approval rates exceeds thresholds. Violations trigger an automated rollback to the previous approved version.

The entire evidence package — policy evaluation results, scan reports, DPIA, model card, signed review records — is stored in a WORM-compliant S3 bucket and referenced in the EU AI Act technical file. The bank can produce a complete audit trail within 4 hours of a regulatory request.
Treating gate approval as a one-time event:

compliance requirements apply to every version of a system. Teams that obtain a DPO sign-off for v1 and assume it covers v2 (even if v2 changes only the prompt) accumulate undisclosed compliance drift.
rather than an immutable digest (SHA-256 of the container image, model weights hash) cannot prove that what was approved is what was deployed.
a scan report that can be modified after the fact provides no assurance. Evidence must be cryptographically signed at creation time.

pre-deployment gates confirm the system is compliant when deployed. They do not catch compliance drift that emerges from real traffic patterns (e.g., users providing PII that gets logged, or a retrieval corpus that expands to include sensitive documents).
Human review gates with no SLA:
manual review without a defined SLA creates unbounded deployment latency and incentivizes teams to route around the gate.
Policy authoring by a single team:
compliance policy that only security engineers understand cannot be validated by legal or the DPO. Rego policies should be readable by the compliance team and tested with examples they provide.

applying the full high-risk gate stack to feature branches and draft PRs destroys developer velocity. Gates should activate on promotion events (merge to protected branches, staging → production promotions), not every commit.

a gate that only blocks, with no defined exception process, pushes teams to disable the gate rather than engage the compliance process. Every gate should have a documented exception path with additional evidence requirements.
Rego files and policy data (approved regions list, threshold values) live in the same repository as the AI system, so policy changes go through the same PR review process as code changes.
). Write test cases for both pass and fail paths; a policy with no tests is a policy with unknown behavior.
always evaluate the SHA-256 digest of the model weights, prompt configuration, and container image. Never evaluate
Separate gate configuration from gate logic:

thresholds, approver lists, and approved-region lists are configuration. Evaluation logic is policy. Configuration changes (adding a region) should not require policy review; policy changes (adding a check) require more scrutiny.
Define SLAs for human review gates:
publish SLAs (e.g., DPO review ≤ 5 business days for medium-risk, legal review ≤ 3 business days) and track compliance with the SLA in the dashboard.
before making a new gate blocking, run it in
(evaluate but do not block) for a sprint. Measure false-positive rate and tune before enforcing.

the gate runner should produce a machine-readable evidence manifest (JSON or SPDX) listing every artifact hash and its location in the evidence store. Human reviewers consume this manifest rather than hunting for artifacts.
Align gate tiers to risk, not compliance team preference:
gate overhead should be proportionate to risk. A gate that treats a typo fix in documentation with the same scrutiny as a model retrain signals that the gate is miscalibrated.
Rotate evidence signing keys on a schedule:
signing keys for evidence attestation should have a defined rotation schedule and be stored in a HSM or cloud KMS. A compromised key invalidates all evidence signed with it.

Gate evaluation time directly affects deployment cycle time. Automated gates should complete in under 5 minutes to remain practically useful in a CI loop; the bottleneck is almost always the security scan, not the policy engine.
OPA evaluates policies in microseconds for typical AI metadata payloads. Policy evaluation is not a bottleneck. Bundle caching (
Trivy scanning a 3 GB container image takes 2–4 minutes without a cache and 30–60 seconds with the vulnerability database cached. Cache the Trivy DB in CI (

SHA-256 hashing multi-GB model files is CPU-bound. For large models, compute the hash once at artifact creation time and store it alongside the weights. The gate verifies the stored hash rather than recomputing it.

multiple deployments can be queued for human review simultaneously. The review interface must support concurrent reviews without creating approval order dependencies. Use a queue-based system (one review request per artifact version, not per deployment event).
high-frequency CI pipelines can generate thousands of evidence records per day. Use a partitioned time-series store or an object store with prefix-based indexing (
). Retention policies should match regulatory requirements — EU AI Act Article 12 requires logs for at least 6 months for high-risk systems.
independent checks (security scan, policy evaluation, SBOM generation) can run in parallel within the gate job. Use CI matrix jobs or async task runners to reduce wall-clock gate time.
No single tool covers the full compliance gate stack. Production systems compose from:
the standard policy engine for policy-as-code. Rego policies are evaluated against JSON input (artifact metadata). OPA integrates with Kubernetes admission control, CI pipelines, and API gateways.

Cosign signs container images and attestations. Rekor provides an append-only transparency log for signed attestations. Together they implement Software Supply Chain Levels for Software Artifacts (SLSA) provenance.
generates SBOMs in SPDX and CycloneDX formats from container images and filesystems.
vulnerability scanner for container images, filesystems, and git repositories. Covers CVEs, misconfigurations, secrets, and license compliance.
scans git history and commits for secrets (API keys, certificates, passwords) before they are promoted.
a supply chain security framework that defines a
(evidence of each step). Used to prove that each gate step was executed in the correct order.
compliance management platforms that aggregate evidence from integrated tools (GitHub, AWS, Jira) and automate SOC 2 and ISO 27001 audit preparation.
a tool for writing and enforcing policies against structured configuration files (Kubernetes manifests, Terraform plans, Helm charts) using OPA Rego. Useful for environment configuration gates.
integrate SAST, secret scanning, and dependency scanning natively into PRs, providing gate-like blocking at the earliest pipeline stage.

# Example: collecting artifact metadata for the OPA gate
# scripts/collect_artifact_metadata.py
"""
Collects the artifact metadata JSON consumed by the OPA policy.
Run inside CI after build but before the compliance gate step.
"""
import hashlib
import json
import os
import subprocess
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path


def sha256(path: str) -> str:
    h = hashlib.sha256()
    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(65536), b""):
            h.update(chunk)
    return h.hexdigest()


def main():
    model_card_path = os.environ.get("MODEL_CARD_PATH", "model_card.json")
    dpia_path = os.environ.get("DPIA_PATH", "")
    review_status = os.environ.get("COMPLIANCE_REVIEW_STATUS", "pending")
    review_approver = os.environ.get("COMPLIANCE_REVIEW_APPROVER", "")
    risk_tier = os.environ.get("RISK_TIER", "low")
    deploy_region = os.environ.get("DEPLOY_REGION", "eu-west-1")

    metadata = {
        "artifact": {
            "model_card_hash": sha256(model_card_path) if Path(model_card_path).exists() else "",
            "model_card_signed": Path(f"{model_card_path}.sig").exists(),
            "dpia_completed": bool(dpia_path and Path(dpia_path).exists()),
            "dpia_hash": sha256(dpia_path) if dpia_path and Path(dpia_path).exists() else "",
            "git_sha": subprocess.check_output(
                ["git", "rev-parse", "HEAD"], text=True
            ).strip(),
            "built_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        },
        "system": {
            "risk_tier": risk_tier,
            "name": os.environ.get("SYSTEM_NAME", "unnamed"),
        },
        "deployment": {
            "region": deploy_region,
            "environment": os.environ.get("DEPLOY_ENV", "staging"),
        },
        "review": {
            "status": review_status,
            "approver_id": review_approver,
        },
    }

    Path("artifact_metadata.json").write_text(json.dumps(metadata, indent=2))
    print("artifact_metadata.json written.")
    print(f"Risk tier: {risk_tier}, Region: {deploy_region}, Review: {review_status}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Scales with CI; human gates bottleneck
Pre-deployment static analysis only (no human gate)
Automated checks only; no legal/DPO coverage
Systems where deployment velocity > compliance risk
Detects drift but cannot prevent initial violation
Broad framework coverage but limited AI-specific policies
Infrastructure configuration only; not model/prompt specific
The compliance gate system itself is a trust anchor — if it can be bypassed or tampered with, its regulatory value is zero. Several properties must be protected:
, Jenkinsfile) with branch protection rules that require the compliance gate job to pass before merge. Restrict who can modify pipeline files to a small set of trusted maintainers.
OPA policy files must be version-controlled and any change to them must pass a
(meta-compliance). Consider signing policy bundles with Cosign and verifying the signature before loading the bundle into OPA.

use a transparency log (Rekor) or a WORM-compliant store for evidence artifacts. Evidence hashes in the decision record should be verified against the stored artifacts before any regulatory submission.

signing keys, evidence store credentials, and review system API tokens must be stored in a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault) with rotation schedules. Gate runner processes should use short-lived OIDC tokens where possible, not long-lived API keys.

the compliance gate is on the critical path of every deployment. OPA can run as a sidecar container or as a centralized cluster. For critical pipelines, run OPA in a replicated configuration and have a documented procedure for emergency deployments if OPA is unavailable (these procedures must themselves be compliance-controlled).

the review interface must authenticate reviewers against the organization's identity provider and enforce that only designated roles can approve each gate type. A DPO gate cannot be approved by an engineer.

decision records must be complete — a gate that records only approvals (not rejections or skips) enables adversarial manipulation by forcing repeated gate invocations until a transient pass occurs. All gate executions, including failures and timeouts, must be logged.
Think of a compliance approval gate as a

between the engineering team and the regulatory/legal environment. The contract specifies what the system must prove before it can take an action (deployment), and what evidence it must leave behind. Like a type system in code, it catches violations early and makes the implicit explicit.
analogous to requiring a building permit before construction begins. You cannot start without it, the permit is specific to what you build, and the permit record exists independently of the building.
analogous to fire safety inspections that occur during building occupancy, not just at construction completion. They catch drift from the approved state.

regulatory requirements translated into machine-readable rules. Just as tax law governs what is deductible, the policy governs what can be deployed. Version-controlling the policy creates a record of what the rules were at any point in time.
the collection of documents a regulator would request. Building it automatically during each deployment means it exists before you need it, not after.

regulators expect scrutiny proportionate to impact. The EU AI Act's risk tier structure directly maps to gate intensity. Applying maximum scrutiny uniformly destroys the economics of AI deployment without commensurate compliance benefit.

The key mental shift is from compliance as a periodic audit activity ("we will get audited once a year") to compliance as a continuous engineering property ("every deployment is compliant by construction"). The gate is the mechanism that makes this shift operational.
that verify AI systems meet regulatory, legal, and organizational requirements before deployment — and produce immutable evidence of that verification.

The EU AI Act, GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, and financial model risk management frameworks all create specific obligations that map naturally to gate checks: DPIAs, model cards, bias evaluations, security scans, and human sign-off processes.
is the standard tool for policy-as-code in compliance gates. Policies should be version-controlled, tested, and readable by the compliance team.

Risk-based gating applies gate intensity proportional to system risk: automated-only gates for low-risk systems, human review gates for medium and high-risk systems. The EU AI Act's risk tier framework maps directly to this structure.
Pre-deployment gates confirm compliance at deployment time;
detect compliance drift during operation. Both are necessary for systems subject to ongoing regulatory obligations.
and stored in a tamper-evident system (Rekor, WORM-compliant storage). Unsigned evidence provides no regulatory assurance.
to be operationally viable. Gates without SLAs create unbounded deployment latency.
The gate system itself is a security boundary: protect pipeline configuration files, sign policy bundles, use short-lived credentials, and log all gate executions — including failures and skips.
: every deployment leaves behind a complete audit trail, making regulatory responses fast and reliable rather than expensive and reconstructed.
